preFix.title: Making a leather bifold card case
preFix.subtitle: Cut, assemble, and burnish a slim two-fold cardholder
preFix.excerpt: How to make a slim bifold card case from vegetable-tanned leather: cutting the panels accurately, skiving the fold edge to reduce bulk, punching and hand-stitching with a saddle stitch, and burnishing the edges to a finished surface.
preFix.sourceNotes: Al Stohlman The Art of Hand Sewing Leather. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A bifold card case is a good first leatherwork project. It uses one piece of
and needs no specialist tools beyond the basics. It teaches the core skills of
, stitching, and edge finishing. A finished case holds four to six cards and folds to roughly 95 x 65 x 5 mm.

Vegtan leather, 1 to 1.5 mm thick, enough for three pieces: an outer body (200 x 95 mm) and two inner pocket panels (90 x 75 mm each). A steel ruler and leatherwork knife for straight cuts. A stitching chisel and mallet for punching stitch holes. Linen or polyester thread at 0.8 mm. Two blunt-tipped needles for saddle stitch. An edge beveller to remove the corner from every cut edge, and a leather burnisher with wood gum or beeswax to finish the edges.
Step 1, cut and skive the panels

Cut all three panels to the dimensions above using the steel ruler and leatherwork knife. Skive along the top edge of each pocket panel (the open edge that cards slide over) to thin it to approximately 0.5 mm; a bevelled edge here prevents the card pocket from feeling thick and stiff. Run the edge beveller along every cut edge on both faces of all three panels.
Step 2, punch and stitch the pocket panels

Position each pocket panel on the outer body: one on the left half, one on the right. Mark the stitch line 3 mm from the bottom and side edges of each pocket panel, then punch the stitch holes at 3 mm spacing using the stitching chisel and mallet through both layers. Saddle-stitch each pocket: thread both needles on one length of thread, pass one needle through the first hole, pull through to the midpoint, then cross both needles through each subsequent hole from opposite sides.
Step 3, fold and finish the edges

Score lightly along the centre fold line on the flesh side with the point of the leatherwork knife, then fold the case closed. Dampen the fold line slightly with water and press flat to set the fold. Burnish all cut edges: apply a small amount of wood gum or beeswax with a finger, then rub the leather burnisher back and forth along the edge at speed until the fibres compress and the edge becomes smooth and dense. Well-burnished edges are the mark of a finished piece over a rough prototype.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
